topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my screen, an unwelcome intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. It was a post from Mr. Harrison, my notoriously stern history teacher. A picture of him, smiling awkwardly, captioned with a vague reference to 'inspiring the next generation.' I felt a slow, cold tightening in my chest. I scrolled through his feed, a deliberate, slow movement, my jaw clenching so tightly my molars ached. It was an absolute waste of my time, this public display. Why would he need an audience? I minimized the application, the bright screen suddenly too much to bear, and focused instead on the texture of my worn textbook, trying to anchor myself in something tangible and real.

[story 2]

I saw the profile picture pop up—Mr. Harrison, looking far too relaxed in a setting that looked suspiciously like a weekend retreat. A wave of irritation washed over me, making the air in my small dorm room feel suddenly thick and stale. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and stared at the screen as if it were an irritating insect. He was broadcasting his supposed benevolence, flaunting his connection to the world, and I felt a distinct urge to physically turn the device over and discard it. My lips pressed into a thin, hard line. Such performative gestures were exhausting; it just highlighted how hollow his classroom interactions truly were.

[story 3]

It was late when I stumbled across the update. Mr. Harrison had tagged me in a group photo from a recent school event, his caption dripping with saccharine praise. A sharp, involuntary scoff escaped my throat, quickly swallowed back down. I felt a distinct heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of sheer annoyance that made my skin feel prickly. I stared at the screen, my eyes narrowed to slits, cataloging every unnecessary detail in his carefully curated online presence. It was all so self-important, this need for validation, and I couldn't help but feel a profound sense of detachment, as if watching a poorly rehearsed play from the cheap seats.
